purpose: Classify any permission that operates on the control plane of a cloud resource as Privileged and Risky. Control-plane actions modify the configuration, existence, or access model of a resource; data-plane actions interact with the content inside a resource. This distinction is foundational to NIST SP 800-207 (Zero Trust) and CSA CCM IAM-03.
references: NIST SP 800-207 Section 3.3 - All data sources and computing services are considered resources
CSA CCM v4 IAM-03 - Privileged Access Management
CIS Controls v8 Control 5 - Account Management (privileged vs standard)

classification_model.privileged: A permission is Privileged when it grants the ability to create, modify, delete, or reconfigure a resource or its access controls — i.e., it operates on the resource itself rather than its content.
classification_model.risky: A permission is Risky when misuse or compromise could lead to data loss, service disruption, unauthorised access escalation, or lateral movement. All control-plane actions are inherently Risky.
classification_model.privileged_and_risky: When a permission is both Privileged and Risky it requires compensating controls: MFA enforcement, just-in-time access, and separate approval workflow.

rules.name: Resource Creation
rules.description: Any action that creates a new cloud resource (bucket, VM, role, policy, cluster node) is a control-plane action and is therefore Privileged and Risky.
rules.rationale: Creating resources can establish persistent footholds (backdoor roles, rogue buckets, shadow VMs). Even a read-only principal who can create roles can immediately escalate privilege.
rules.remediation: Require MFA at time of use; restrict to named principals in a break-glass role; log and alert every invocation.

rules.name: Resource Modification / Update
rules.description: Any action that modifies the configuration of an existing resource (update, put, patch, set, modify, replace) is a control-plane action.
rules.rationale: Modifying a resource's configuration can silently disable security controls (e.g., disabling bucket versioning before mass-delete, modifying a firewall rule to open a port).
rules.remediation: Enforce change-management approval; alert on configuration drift; use immutable infrastructure where possible.

rules.name: Resource Deletion
rules.description: Any action that permanently removes a resource or its contents is a control-plane action. Deletion is the highest-impact control-plane verb.
rules.rationale: Deletion is typically irreversible without a backup strategy. A compromised principal with delete rights can cause permanent data loss or service disruption.
rules.remediation: Enable soft-delete / versioning on all critical resources; require dual-control (two approvals) for deletion of production resources; enforce resource lock policies.

rules.name: Access Control / Permission Modification
rules.description: Any action that creates, modifies, or deletes IAM policies, roles, bindings, ACLs, or trust relationships is a control-plane action and is the highest-risk category because it directly affects the permission boundary of other principals.
rules.rationale: The ability to modify access controls is the master key to the entire system. An attacker who gains this permission can silently grant themselves or an accomplice any other permission.
rules.remediation: Restrict to a dedicated IAM-admin role with no other permissions; enforce MFA; require ticket-based approval; log every change with before/after diff.

rules.name: Network / Connectivity Configuration
rules.description: Actions that modify network topology, firewall rules, routing tables, ingress controllers, VPN gateways, or DNS are control-plane actions that can expose resources to the internet or enable lateral movement.
rules.rationale: Network misconfigurations are the most common initial access vector. A single firewall rule change can expose an entire subnet to the internet.
rules.remediation: Treat network changes as code (GitOps); require peer review and CI pipeline validation before applying; alert on any public IP exposure.

rules.name: Encryption Key / Secret Management
rules.description: Actions that create, rotate, disable, or delete encryption keys, secrets, or certificates are control-plane actions with the highest potential for data exposure.
rules.rationale: Compromise of a key management action can decrypt all data protected by that key, or render it permanently unreadable (key deletion). Both are catastrophic.
rules.remediation: Require dual-control for key creation and deletion; enable automatic key rotation; restrict KMS permissions to a single dedicated service account with no other rights.

rules.name: Data-Plane Read (Standard)
rules.description: Actions that read, list, or download the content of a resource (not its configuration) are data-plane operations. These are not inherently Privileged but may become Risky at scale (bulk export).
rules.rationale: Data-plane reads operate within the established access boundary. Risk depends on data sensitivity, not the action itself.
rules.remediation: Ensure data classification tags are enforced; monitor for anomalous read volume (potential exfiltration).

rules.name: Data-Plane Write (Standard)
rules.description: Actions that write, upload, or append content to a resource are data-plane operations. They are Risky (data integrity / injection risk) but not Privileged (they cannot modify the resource's access model).
rules.rationale: Data-plane writes can introduce malicious content, corrupt state, or overwrite legitimate data. They require integrity controls but do not grant privilege escalation paths.
rules.remediation: Enable object versioning; enforce content validation at ingest; monitor for unusual write patterns.
